data.title: Nestlé: Improving Growth Quality Reinforce Our Bull Case
data.description:
We last commented on Nestlé (NSRGY) (NSRGF) (NEST:CA) after the Q4 and Fiscal Year 2025 release. This was not a lucky time (Fig. 1), given the escalation in the Middle East and, as a reminder, the company was also impacted by the infant formula recall in January 2026. Despite that, we remained constructive on Nestlé's equity story.

This was supported by the company's brand momentum and stepped-up marketing expenses that stabilized market share. Although 2026 profitability expectations were revised down, the company has many levers to implement, such as disciplined cost management and portfolio optimization. Considering extremely negative Wall Street expectations and Nestlé's economic moat, we reiterated our buy rating.

Q1 results came in solid, and we also participated in the post-earnings call following the sell-side session, allowing us to share additional insights with our readers/investors.

Fig 1

Q1 Results and Our Positive Stance

The company reported only its sales figures by product categories and geography. Nestlé also confirmed its 2026 outlook.

The company achieved organic growth of +3.5%, driven by internal growth of +1.2 % and a price impact of +2.3%. Growth was well supported in Emerging Markets (Fig. 2). That said, looking at the press release, in Q1, the company's top-line sales reached CHF 21.3 billion. This represents a 5.7% decrease, mainly due to FX.

Fig 2

Why are we still Positive?

There are many positive updates to report:

Firstly, the company expects a modest impact from the Middle East, with only a limited impact on supply chain disruptions and commodity inflation.

Nestlé's Q1 2026 results provide strong validation of its new and more focused strategy. After a year in 2025 defined by resilience, we see the Q1 2026 update as a crucial positive inflection point. The company’s growth shifted to the Real Internal Growth (RIG). Organic growth of 3.5% was stable versus Q4, but the composition improved dramatically. RIG accelerated to 1.2% (Fig. 3) while pricing moderated to 2.3%. This means that Nestlé is winning consumers on volume, not just price.

Fig 3

The strategic decision to focus on four powerhouse categories (Coffee, Petcare, Nutrition, Food & Snacks) is yielding results. Strong performance in Coffee and improving trends in Food & Snacks are driving this RIG acceleration. Additionally, we see future benefits from lower cocoa and coffee prices. This has weighed on Nestlé's performance in 2025; however, we will see headwinds towards the end of 2026, with full impact in 2027. This will provide margin expansion ahead.

Going back to the recent IMF recall, the company reported that product availability is now back to normal. Sales are down by approximately 10% due to lower consumption, but the company already reported early signs of improvement.

On a positive note, Nestlé is also tackling younger consumers. The company started partnering with influencers such as Dua Lipa for Nespresso. This will bring new clients to Nespresso. We also positively report the recent partnership between KitKat and Formula One.

Management maintained its full-year guidance despite a strong Q1 and new geopolitical risks (Fig. 3). This should be viewed as a classic "de-risking" move by a prudent leadership team, suggesting confidence in meeting targets despite known headwinds such as the infant formula recall. Additionally, given the 90-basis-point sales drag from the IMF recall, Nestlé would have reported RIG and OG above 2% and 4% on an underlying basis.

Earnings Changes and Valuation

Last time, we reported room to grow in 2026 guidance. We already highlighted FX headwinds, so there was no negative surprise in this quarter. On the other hand, we see margin improvement ahead. In 2025, the company took substantial pricing on coffee and cocoa. This was due to cost inflation; however, Nestlé did not fully offset it. With higher pricing ahead, lower coffee and cocoa raw material prices will drive a margin uplift. We still believe this will not be the case in 2026; however, we prefer to anticipate this trend rather than track the company’s share price. Considering no change in management guidance, we confirm our assumptions for top-line sales and EBIT of CHF 89 billion and CHF 14.6 billion, respectively. With a corporate tax rate of 22%, JV income for CHF 1.5 billion and interest payments of CHF 1.45 billion, our net profit reached CHF 11.38 billion. Given the current number of shares, our EPS is CHF 4.41, and with a P/E target of 20x, we continue to see upside at the current price. Our target price is set at CHF 88.2 ($112 in ADR).

Risks

Nestlé's downside risks include negative FX evolution. This was a 5% drag to sales in Q1. The Middle East conflict will have some impact on commodity and distribution costs, and possibly on consumer behavior, but it is too early to know the full extent. Guidance reflects macro/market uncertainty, which offsets the impact of the IMF. Additionally, we report higher inflation and clients’ preferences towards non-branded products (private label). Other risks include ongoing disposal prices and restructuring activities. Nestlé is targeting significant cost savings; a failure in this delivery could trigger a negative share-price reaction.

Conclusion

Q1 2026 results show a clear improvement in the quality of Nestlé's growth. The company reiterated its 2026 outlook; however, adjusting for the infant formula recall, Nestlé could have reached an OG of 3-4%. We are positive on COGS evolution for the company’s main product category, and with a return to RIG growth, we remain buyers of the Swiss food giant.

data.title: SoftBank Group: A Discounted AI HoldCo With Several Paths To Value Realization
data.description:
Investment Thesis

SoftBank Group (SFTBY) remains, in my view, an undervalued stock because the market still looks at the company as a funding vehicle with a complex structure, while not fully taking into account the value of the assets it owns. The current equity valuation appears to be discounted because of financing concerns tied to the AI investments, the bridge-loan situation, and the HoldCo discount.

Yet the underlying assets — Arm, the Vision Fund stakes, PayPay, SoftBank Corp., and other holdings — appear to be worth more than what the public market is assigning to the company today. According to the latest IR document, NAV per ordinary share was ¥5,427 as of December 31, 2025. Using USD/JPY around 158.58 and then converting to the ADR ratio, that comes to roughly $17.11 per SFTBY ADR, versus a current ADR price near $14.74. So, the stock is already trading below the official published NAV on an ADR basis, and if we also take the rerating of the underlying assets into consideration, we can see further upside.

Another point is that the official NAV is not the latest valuation. Since December 2025, Arm has been rerated much higher. According to Reuters, Arm’s market capitalization is around $169.2B, versus the much lower Arm mark inside SoftBank’s December framework. That means the true NAV for SoftBank is likely higher than the published NAV, which already exceeds the ADR price. Therefore, the stock looks undervalued not only because it sits below published NAV, but also because the market is still not giving proper credit to how much NAV has likely risen as Arm has rerated. Also, now that PayPay has become publicly visible, the opacity issue around PayPay is no longer there.

Company Background & Evolution

SoftBank’s investments, over time, have shifted from telecom and internet sectors toward a much more concentrated AI and technology focus. In the latest phase, that change has accelerated. Reuters reported that SoftBank had invested more than $30B in OpenAI by early 2026 and was considering investing more. The company has also tied itself closely to the Stargate AI infrastructure initiative. Therefore, the stock is increasingly being viewed as a proxy for the AI sector, but with a financing problem attached to it. This new profile explains both the discount and the upside potential for the stock.

Where SoftBank Stands Today

As of the latest official SOTP disclosure, SoftBank’s holdings are made up of Arm, SoftBank Vision Fund 1, Vision Fund 2, LatAm Funds, SoftBank Corp., a smaller T-Mobile stake, and a miscellaneous Others bucket. SoftBank’s own IR shows that, as of December 31, 2025, NAV was ¥30.93T, NAV per share was ¥5,427, and LTV was 20.6%. On an ADR-equivalent basis, that NAV per share works out to roughly $17.11 per SFTBY ADR. This means that the market is not valuing all of the assets fairly. That discount makes the bull case stronger if Arm continues to rerate.

The largest change since December 2025 is Arm itself. SoftBank’s investor deck used an Arm equity value of about $100.9B at the end of December 2025. Reuters now shows Arm’s market value at around $169.2B. This means the officially published NAV is now outdated on the single most important asset. The rerating of Arm’s valuation, at roughly 70% higher, is itself estimated to bring meaningful upside.

Why The Stock Trades At A Discount

There are a few clear reasons why the market still applies a large discount:

First, the financing risk is real. Reuters reported that SoftBank secured a $40B bridge loan in March 2026 to fund further OpenAI investment. It then raised $1.5B in dollar bonds and €1.75B in euro bonds in April, partly to refinance debt and repay that bridge. CreditSights still viewed the underlying asset value as strong, but estimated a $35.7B funding shortfall. This is the major reason the stock is trading at a discount: investors are concerned that SoftBank might be stretching itself too far in its AI investments.

Second, SoftBank’s structure is complicated. SoftBank’s own NAV page says the published NAV is before tax, and the asset values are shown after certain financing adjustments. In other words, investors are being asked to capitalize a HoldCo with taxes, debt, asset-backed financing, and private-asset marks sitting inside the same framework. That almost always creates a discount, especially when some of the largest lines are not liquid public equities.

Third, there is opacity around the private assets. Vision Fund 1, Vision Fund 2, and the LatAm Funds are not worthless, but they are not marked daily. The market naturally applies a haircut to these assets. This means the market is unwilling to pay full look-through value for them until they are either made more transparent or monetized and utilized.

Why I Think There Is Value In The Company

In my view, the market is over-penalizing the funding problem (relative to the asset base):

Arm is the best asset. Reuters reported that Arm’s latest AI-oriented product push could drive roughly $15B of annual revenue within five years. Whether or not one fully believes that number, the important point is that Arm is no longer being valued just as a mature licensing business; it has also become a central part of the AI sector. SoftBank owns the majority of that asset, and the stock market is marking it higher in real time. SoftBank therefore has a large liquid and strategically important asset inside the structure that is still not being fully reflected in its parent’s current market cap.

PayPay is another useful asset. Reuters reported that PayPay priced its IPO at $16 per share, raising about $880M, and that SoftBank still owned more than 90% after the offering. Reuters also reported that PayPay’s initial valuation reached about $12.7B. That matters because PayPay is no longer a private asset. It is now a public asset whose value we can calculate in real time. In my view, investors are still not fully understanding the upside they could get if part of PayPay is monetized, for example through an equity carve-out, and the capital raised is used to narrow the funding gap.

SoftBank has already shown that it is willing to monetize some of its holdings. Its own filings say it sold 56.9 million T-Mobile shares for $12.73B between June and December 2025. That is a useful precedent because it means management is aware of this monetization route. Therefore, I do not see a very large issue if management chooses to pursue further monetization of other parts that are relatively non-core.

Catalysts

The specific catalysts are as follows:

The first catalyst is continued Arm rerating. If Arm keeps trading stronger because of AI optimism, then SoftBank’s implied NAV will keep rising accordingly. That is probably the most direct catalyst in the whole thesis. Arm’s new AI chip strategy and revenue guidance further give me confidence that the rerating can continue rather than reverse.

The second catalyst is PayPay monetization. Now that PayPay is public, any partial sale, even a small one, can help narrow the funding gap or support other shareholder-friendly steps. Therefore, investors can now see a real source of liquidity, and the earlier opacity and valuation issues are largely gone.

The third catalyst is further portfolio simplification. More T-Mobile monetization, some exits from listed positions, whether full or partial, and/or a cleaner balance sheet through deleveraging could all help narrow the HoldCo discount.

Risks

The biggest risk is that SoftBank may keep increasing its AI commitments faster than it monetizes assets, which would keep widening the funding gap. The second risk is that Arm might start declining in market value, in which case the stock would lose NAV support and the overall company valuation would come under pressure. The third risk is that the Vision Fund valuation does not improve, which would leave investors treating those assets as beleaguered rather than healthy. That would further reduce valuation.

Thesis Summary

The thesis is that SoftBank is a discounted AI HoldCo where the market is still focusing too much on the funding problem — financing and bridging — and paying too little attention to the value of the assets underneath. That creates a dislocation between price and underlying value. The current discount exists for understandable reasons, but I think it is now too wide because Arm has rerated, PayPay has become public, monetization options exist, and the market is still valuing the company as if the financing burden will overwhelm the value of all the assets.

In my view, the facts are that the assets are large enough that, if management executes well, the funding concern can be addressed relatively easily, thereby improving valuation. The current ADR price of about $14.74 compared with official published NAV of about $17.11 per ADR, and that official NAV does not yet take into consideration Arm’s recent rerating, shows a clear discount. Further, due to the rerating in the value of its assets, I think the stock can reasonably rerate toward roughly $19.5 per ADR in a base case and toward about $22.1 per ADR in a stronger bull case, as the market starts giving fuller credit to Arm’s rerating, PayPay’s public value, and SoftBank’s broader asset base. Thus, there is approximately 30% to 50% upside relative to the current ADR price.

Conclusion

SoftBank is not a clean company, and that is exactly why the opportunity exists. The stock is cheap because it is complex and tied to very large AI funding commitments. But those issues can be addressed because of the presence of real assets. Arm is at a higher valuation than it was at the last official NAV update. PayPay is now public — the opacity issue has disappeared — and asset monetization has precedent, so there is a path to liquidity. Also, the market is still paying more attention to bridge financing problems and HoldCo complexity than to the value of the parts. That, in my view, is the cornerstone of the bullish thesis. If Arm continues to rerate and funding gaps begin to ease, SFTBY should see the capital-structure problem ease and, in turn, an appreciation in the share price.

data.title: Microsoft Is A Value Trap, OpenAI Dependence Could Backfire
data.description:
Introduction

Microsoft Corporation (MSFT) is trading at its most conservative valuation in years, hovering around a 25.2x forward P/E ratio, almost 20% lower than the 5-year average of 31.3x. Many analysts across both Wall Street and Seeking Alpha are considering this a generational buying opportunity – 53 out of 56 Wall Street analysts in the last 90 days rate MSFT a Buy or Strong Buy.

While many consider MSFT an excellent opportunity at the current valuation, I hold a contrarian view and consider MSFT to be a value trap. I am initiating coverage on MSFT with a Sell rating because of a high disruption risk to MSFT’s software segment from agentic AI and a dangerous over-reliance on OpenAI (OPENAI), wherein the negative effects of this partnership are brought to light when looking at the dynamics of CapEx spending, revenue quality, and revenue backlog.

Background

MSFT’s drawdown reached a staggering -35.9% from its 52-week high to its 52-week low. This drawdown was caused by a variety of factors: persistent conflict in the Middle East weighing on global risk appetite, reports of a hiring freeze across MSFT’s cloud sales groups, and, most crucially, a 66% year-over-year surge in CapEx, much of which is tied to OpenAI. MSFT has since rebounded +17% since its 52-week lows.

MSFT’s fiscal second-quarter 2026 results revealed $37.5 billion in capital spending, primarily directed at the buildout of data centers and the procurement of high-end GPUs to support its Azure AI workloads. For the full fiscal year 2026, Microsoft is on pace to deploy between $110 billion and $120 billion in capital.

The underlying concern is that MSFT is building a bridge to nowhere if the software layer does not monetize as rapidly as the hardware depreciates. While Azure continues to grow at a headline rate of 39%, the market is increasingly focused on the return on hardware rather than raw revenue growth.

Anthropic Dominance

The most significant threat to MSFT’s long-term enterprise dominance is not a direct competitor in the cloud space but the shift in how work is performed. While this article has been written on Microsoft Word, and I use PowerPoint and Excel on a day-to-day basis for my day job, I think the real threat to MSFT is not the ease of replicating enterprise tools by so-called “vibe coders” on Anthropic’s (ANTHRO) Claude, but rather what is being dubbed as seat compression.

MSFT’s business model can be considered to be dependent on the number of subscribers. With 450 million commercial Microsoft 365 users, the company’s revenue is a direct function of human headcount. However, the rise of agentic systems like Claude Cowork allows a single AI agent to perform the tasks of multiple human employees, leading to seat compression. When an enterprise can replace a 10 person audit team with a single agentic workflow, Microsoft loses nine seats of M365, nine seats of Teams Premium, and nine seats of Copilot.

This is not just some theoretical risk 50 years out in the future. Surveys indicate that 40% of IT budgets are being reallocated from traditional SaaS subscriptions to agentic platforms and LLM token usage.

While Microsoft has strengthened its ties to OpenAI, the enterprise world is gravitating toward Anthropic. Currently, 44% of enterprises are using Anthropic in production, a figure that rises to over 63% when including those in the testing phase. Additionally, as shown in the chart above, the growth of Anthropic far outpaces the pace of growth of any other AI model. While OpenAI had a huge first-mover advantage, Anthropic has pretty much already caught up to OpenAI.

Even MSFT has implicitly acknowledged Anthropic’s superiority. In its latest "Researcher" agent for M365 Copilot, MSFT introduced a feature called "Critique." In this workflow, OpenAI’s GPT drafts a response, but Anthropic’s Claude is used to review it for accuracy, completeness, and citation quality. This creates a humiliating irony for MSFT: its own flagship AI assistant relies on a rival's model to correct the mistakes of its primary partner.

OpenAI Dependence

MSFT’s investment in OpenAI was initially considered a masterstroke of corporate strategy. However, I believe this partnership has turned into a significant risk of counterparty concentration. The $625 billion backlog cited as evidence of Azure’s strength is not what it seems.

The fact that 45% of MSFT’s cloud backlog, equivalent to $281 billion, is tied to OpenAI commitments is a double-edged sword. While it ensures a base level of Azure utilization, it also means that MSFT’s growth is fundamentally tied to a company that currently shows $14 billion a year in losses.

This $281 billion number tied to OpenAI alone also appears quite absurd when compared to the backlog of other hyperscalers – Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN), tied to Anthropic, and Alphabet Inc. (GOOG)(GOOGL), tied to Google Gemini, both show a backlog of less than $250 billion. I believe the market is likely already partially pricing in the risk that OpenAI may be unable to meet its long-term financial obligations, which is not unreasonable to assume.

Perhaps the most damning aspect of the OpenAI relationship is what it reveals about MSFT’s own R&D failure. While Google successfully developed Gemini, which leads in deep reasoning and multimodal analysis, and xAI produced Grok, which dominates real-time social data analysis, Microsoft has failed to produce a marketable foundation model.

MSFT is currently forced to play the role of foundry for other labs’ models because it hasn’t built its own foundation model. GOOG, for example, have their own models, infrastructure, and custom silicon, allowing them to optimize the entire stack for cost and synergies across the Google ecosystem. MSFT, by contrast, is stuck in a margin sandwich where it bears all the CapEx risk but captures a fraction of the AI value-add.

Financial Performance

MSFT’s financial performance and growth in recent quarters are excellent – there is no denying this. In FY26 Q2 earnings, MSFT showed a year-over-year growth of 16.7% and quarter-over-quarter growth of 4.6%. Furthermore, gross margins have remained stable over the last few quarters, hovering around slightly less than 70%. MSFT’s balance sheet is healthy, showing cash reserves of almost $90 billion. MSFT also doesn’t suffer from the same private credit risk overhang that companies like Oracle Corporation (ORCL) face due to their excellent balance sheet.

Microsoft is raising commercial prices globally on July 1, 2026. The price increases hit virtually every major plan, with Enterprise suites like M365 E3 and E5 increasing by 8% and 5% respectively, while frontline plans like F1 and F3 are seeing jumps of up to 33%. While this sounds great for top-line growth at first glance, I believe that, from an enterprise perspective, this is a forced upsell because MSFT is justifying this price hike through bundling in Security Copilot and Intune capabilities into the core plans, which many companies do not need at all.

I think this strategy is likely to accelerate the trend of license rightsizing, where companies move users to lower-tier plans, in turn actually negating the effects of the price hike or even resulting in lower revenues than current levels. Out of MSFT’s 450 million users, just 3.3% paid for Copilot.

Valuation

MSFT is trading at a trailing P/E ratio much lower than GOOG and AMZN – it seems to be the most undervalued among the 3 at first glance. In fact, MSFT is currently trading at a trailing P/E ratio 32% lower than its 1-year highs. However, I am seeing a different picture.

AMZN, linked to Anthropic, is a clear leader & trades at the highest multiple because of their significant exposure to Anthropic. Much of AWS’s revenue is tied to Anthropic, which I consider to be much higher quality revenue because of the simple fact that, as previously established, Anthropic is a clear leader in terms of real enterprise adoption. GOOG, with Gemini trading at the second highest multiple, has also proven to be quite successful – they’ve had a rally because of how well received Gemini 3 was. GOOG’s AI models are also synergistic with the rest of GOOG’s business, with the potential to adopt AI across their entire ecosystem.

MSFT, on the other hand, trades at the lowest multiple of the three. This can be attributed to the fact that they do not have their own foundational model. As previously discussed, they are bearing a large portion of the CapEx risk without much of the AI value-add of having a foundational model. They are reliant on third-party models to be integrated across the MSFT ecosystem, leading to margin pressure. Further worsening the case for MSFT is the potential for a revenue decline from license rightsizing.

Additionally, sentiment for software companies is terrible at the moment, largely due to the potential of disruption from AI. ServiceNow, Inc. (NOW), for example, raised their guidance and showed a 22% increase in revenue year-over-year, yet shares still dropped over 20% from $103 prior to earnings.

While MSFT seems the most undervalued of the 3 hyperscalers compared, I see further decline in MSFT’s valuation – this can be attributed to multiple compression due to their declining standing in the AI race & the worsening of sentiment for software businesses and the potential decline in revenue, as previously discussed.

Upside Risks

MSFT’s current cloud growth is limited by supply, not demand. CFO Amy Hood has signaled that Azure could reaccelerate in the second half of fiscal 2026 as new capacity comes online. If MSFT can bring its data centers online and fill them with non-OpenAI customers at high margins, the stock could easily trade back to its historical 30x multiple.

Additionally, OpenAI is reportedly preparing for an IPO in late 2026 or early 2027 at a valuation approaching $1 trillion. If this listing is successful, Microsoft’s ~27% stake would become a highly liquid asset worth over $270 billion – this is a significant amount able to account for much of the CapEx being spent to build data centers.

Conclusion

To conclude, I believe that MSFT, while appearing to be undervalued, has significant room for continued decline in the share price. I attribute this decline to potential disruption from agentic systems developed by companies like Anthropic, leading to seat compression and to MSFT’s significant over-reliance on OpenAI, which I believe to provide low quality revenue and falls behind competitors. I also believe that MSFT’s revenue could decline from license rightsizing, due to planned hikes on MSFT’s services justified by lumping in Copilot, which itself has a very low adoption rate.

data.title: Elmet Group: Some Questions On This Rare And Critical Materials Business
data.description:
Shares of Elmet Group (ELMT) have seen a strong IPO offering, which is no surprise, as the company positions itself as a company that secures US critical component supply chains, reducing reliance on foreign (notably Chinese) imports or supplies.

The company is benefiting from a strong strategic positioning, and while trading at a mere 2.5 times sales multiple (which looks low in relation to this positioning), these appear to be lower-margin businesses with strong immediate growth seen.

With no quarter growth cadences reported, no preliminary first-quarter results reported (when prices of some of these critical materials jumped), I am a bit left in the dark about the operating momentum here.

This makes me keen to learn more about the performance post the offering, yet the lack of transparency and high earnings multiples implied here make me very cautious, and prevent me from getting on board at this time.

Other serial value compounders and electronic distributors are discussed more extensively at Value In Corporate Events.

Securing US Critical Component Supply Chain

Elmet has a mission to secure the US critical components supply chain, involving critical materials, processes, engineering, and manufacturing. The company provides precision-engineered components and high-energy systems in growth markets. These solutions are provided to aerospace, defense, government, industrial, medical, semiconductor, and energy industries.

The focus of such products is that they are critical, including products like tungsten, molybdenum, and niobium. Often these involve complex engineering processes such as high-level radio frequency, such as plasma generation, radar, etc.

The business is organized across two segments: a critical materials components business and an engineered microwave products business. The former segment includes products like foil, powder, plates, balance weights, cubes, etc. Engineered microwave products include microwave generators and systems, waveguide components, and ultra-high vacuum. Critical material and components comprise about 85% of sales, being the dominant segment in all this.

The company provides these products to governmental and high-end customer clientele, with the company claiming to be the only vertically integrated engineering manufacturer of tungsten and molybdenum. It is the combination of strong positioning, which capitalized on growth prospects, a unique positioning of integrated operations regarding the engineering and sales of rare commodities, and others.

Valuation & IPO Thoughts

Elmet aimed to sell 7.7 million shares in a preliminary price range between $12 and $14 per share, with pricing set at the higher end of the range. All the shares were sold by the company, which raised the size of the offering to 8.6 million shares, thereby ending up fetching $120 million in gross proceeds; that is excluding the greenshoe option.

With nearly 29 million shares outstanding following the offering, the company is awarded a $406 million equity valuation at the offer price. The company operates with a net cash position just over $50 million, resulting in an operating asset valuation of around $350 million.

In 2025, the company grew sales by some 6% to just over $201 million, with gross margins reported at just over 20%. Operating profits actually fell from $14 million to $12 million, as net earnings came in at just a few million following interest expenses, taxes, but also non-controlling interests taking a bit out of these earnings.

The momentum does not appear to be too strong, yet over the same period of time, the backlog rose from $70 million to $96 million over this twelve-month period. Growth was driven by a 20% increase in the aerospace, defense and government segments, now making up over 40% of sales. Industrial sales rose by a percent to $66 million, with medical, semiconductor, and energy all being smaller and declining segments.

Shares of the company rose by more than a quarter to nearly $18 per share on the first day of trading. This move added well over a hundred million to the market value of the firm, with operating assets valued at nearly half a billion. That values the business at just 2.5 times sales reported in 2025, yet operating margins are reported at just 6%, leaving the valuation unable to be supported by the current earnings power.

Moreover, disclosure on the growth cadence has been very limited, although sales could likely grow due to rapidly rising prices of some key elements, notably tungsten here. This is the result of export restrictions in China, the dominant global producer here.

Final Take

Risks in this offering and the business might be above average. Other than exposure to defense markets, the company is depending heavily on both the availability and pricing of key inputs, notably tungsten, molybdenum, and niobium. Other risks include reliance on its CEO Peter Anania, complex and capital-intensive manufacturing, tariffs, a limited supplier base, etc.

With some of these price adjustments, I expect a real jump in first-quarter sales, which does not necessarily translate into an earnings jump, but unfortunately, no preliminary results have been released just yet, even though the quarter has long ended.

This makes me a bit cautious, but curious to learn about the sales momentum here, as well as margin development post the offering for what is positioned as a great business.

data.title: EICA: Eagle Point Income's Remaining Term Preferred
data.description:
Introduction

Eagle Point Income Company Inc. (EIC) invests in Collateralized Loan Obligations, known as CLOs, which comes with this definition:

  • CLOs are securities backed by a pool of debt, typically corporate loans with low credit ratings, which are divided into different tranches to appeal to various investor risk appetites.
  • CLOs are divided into debt tranches and equity tranches, with debt tranches being paid first and carrying lower risk, while equity tranches offer higher potential returns but come with higher risk as they are paid out last.
  • CLOs are actively managed, allowing managers to buy and sell loans within the pool, which aims to optimize returns and minimize losses, offering diversity and potential for higher-than-average yields to investors.
  • Historically, CLO tranches have experienced lower default rates than corporate bonds, but they remain complex and are generally suited for large institutional investors due to inherent risks.
  • The creation of a CLO involves establishing a capital structure, raising capital, purchasing loans, and managing a special purpose vehicle to protect investors, culminating in repayment structured by tranches.

Source: investopedia.

I last wrote about the term preferred offered by Eagle Point Income shortly after they issued their third one (article link). Here, I review the remaining one, the Eagle Point Income Company Inc 5.0% Series A Term Preferred Stock due 10/31/2026 (EICA) as both the Eagle Point Income Company Inc 7.75% Series B Term Preferred Stock due 2028 (EICB) and the Eagle Point Income Company Inc.8.0% Series C Term Preferred Stock Due 04/30/2029 (EICC) were redeemed since then, which I take as a sign of corporate financial strength.

The difference between preferred and term preferred is the second come with a termination, or maturity, date. It is also classified as debt on the balance sheet.

While a secure 5% yield and YTM, I think most investors would be willing to accept some price risk and gain 2-3% greater yield, thus a Sell rating is assigned. EICA worth holding until it matures in October in place of current CD or MMA yields.

Eagle Point Income Company review

Seeking Alpha provides this overview of EIC:

The Company’s primary investment objective is to generate high current income, with a secondary objective to generate capital appreciation. The Company seeks to achieve its investment objectives by investing primarily in junior debt tranches of collateralized loan obligations, or “CLOs,” that are collateralized by a portfolio consisting primarily of below investment grade U.S. senior secured loans with a large number of distinct underlying borrowers across various industry sectors.

This is how the EIC managers describe their investment strategy.

With an emphasis on assessing the skill of CLO collateral managers and analyzing CLO structures, the Adviser believes that its approach is more akin to a private equity style investment approach than to the typical process used by many fixed income investors.

The goal of the investment process is to outperform the CLO market generally over the long-term. Our investment process involves proactive sourcing and identification of investment opportunities in both the primary and secondary market.

Source: eaglepointincome: strategy.

The next chart shows the structure of the CLO market and where EIC places their focus.

Since 1994, less than 1% of the BB-rated tranches have defaulted. Lastly, here is a summary of the CLO portfolio held by EIC.

With the recent redemption of EICC, I might be making assumptions here, but it appears the coverage would be about 5.3X ($380/72). Payment coverage would be near 1X if using the Total GAPP NII as the payments would have been deducted.

Remaining Term Preferred

I have never seen "variable" listed in the Tax rate box. Compared to the non-cumulative preferreds issued by most financial institutions, these are all cumulative when it comes to payments. Eagan Jones gives all three a BBB rating, though other readers discount that firm. EIC calculated their average loan rating to be B+/B, or mid-level “junk.”

This table compares the key features of each issue.

Factor EICA EICB EICC
Issue date 10/18/21 7/13/21 3/28/24
Issue size 1.22m 1.13m 1.22m
Coupon 5.00% 7.75% 8.00%
Call date 10/30/23 7/30/25 4/3/26
Maturity date 10/30/26 7/31/28 4/30/29
Price $24.99 Called 11/13/25 Called 3/31/26
Yield 5.00%
YTC NA
YTM 5.00%

Covenants require the Term preferred to be redeemed if the assets coverage falls below 200% at the end of a calendar quarter. While a safe 5% yield and YTM, I think most investors would be willing to accept some price risk and gain 2-3% greater yield, thus a Sell rating is assigned.

Portfolio strategy

Owning a Term preferred stock is similar to owning a Note, with one known difference; the Note ranks above the preferred stock in the capital structure. Since few BB-rated issues end up in default, the extra yield a preferred stock should provide over a Note with the same rating to many investors is worth moving down the capital structure.

When comparing preferreds to Notes, other features to consider would be:

  • Note interest payments are usually not skippable and must be made up later. Unless the preferred is cumulative, a missed payment is not required to be made up.
  • Note coupons are less likely to be floating whereas many preferreds coupons are, making the formula used important to understand.
  • If the preferred is not Term in nature, it will not mature. Whether that is a plus or minus differs by investor and why the issue is being held.
  • Income earned could be taxed at different rates, so this comes into play based on the tax status of the account.

data.title: Chart Of The Day: Can Anything Stop These Market Juggernauts?
data.description:
By Mike Larson

Can anything stop the semiconductor stocks? They’re on one of their most powerful runs in years - with one juggernaut in particular standing out this week!

Just look at the MoneyShow Chart of the Day. It’s a weekly chart of Texas Instruments Inc. (“TI”) (TXN) over the last five years. The stock surged as much as 19% yesterday, its biggest one-day rally in a quarter century!

A Texas-Sized Move in “Texan”

An incredibly bullish outlook drove the rally. TI said it would generate $5 billion to $5.4 billion in Q2 sales, far exceeding the $4.8 billion that analysts expected. CEO Haviv Ilan said demand was strong across multiple divisions and geographies, rather than confined to one or two product lines.

TI is more sensitive to manufacturing, industrial, and automotive demand than other chip stocks. It doesn’t make the kind of high-end chips that AI darlings like Nvidia Corp. (NVDA) do.

But it’s still benefiting from the AI boom. That’s because it sells chips that go into data center-related equipment, like gear that regulates power supplies. Sales in that division surged 90% year-over-year in Q1.

Bottom line? TI is now up around 60% year-to-date. The iShares Semiconductor ETF (SOXX) is up 40% YTD. And both are leaving the SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY) - up only 3%-and-change - in the dust. Just like strength in transports by the way, strength in chips is something you want to see if you’re a market bull!

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

Originally published on MoneyShow.com

data.title: Netflix Reigns Supreme With Unmatched Viewership, Global Reach, And Robust Fundamentals
data.description:
Only two months after the publication of my previous coverage, Netflix, Inc. (NFLX) has already delivered 16% returns, which justifies my bullish rating before. With that, I decided to reassess this stock and update it. As I look into it, NFLX still appears to be a buy as entry opportunities and valuation remain in sync. Its fundamentals are still robust, which can support and justify further upside. Technicals adhere to them as the recent selloff widens buying room and shows that bullish signals are still solid.

NFLX Q1 2026: A Solid Start

Amid the intense and rigid streaming wars, more and more websites and apps are emerging to enter and take a portion of the global share. As they try to expand by acquiring films, series, and even books to transform into live video, they also attempt to reach a wider audience. Yet, those who have cemented their status and reputation have remained unmatched with hundreds of millions of viewers. Their wide range of connections and networks continues to help them be ahead of the competition by being the first to get the best possible stories for viewers. One of them is Netflix, Inc., the streaming champion, and you can see how it started the year strong based on its most recent performance.

In Q1 2026, its operating revenue amounted to $12.25B, up by 16.2% YoY and 1.7% QoQ. In only a year, NFLX generated extra revenues of nearly $2B, showing its sustained growth over the years. Now, an investor may be discouraged because its YoY growth decreased from my previous coverage. Also, the QoQ change appears to be slowing. However, we should note the law of big numbers. NFLX is already enormous, and seeing it maintain double-digit revenue growth within the mid-to-upper 10s means a lot. This should not be surprising considering its strong market positioning and solid customer base. It is also impressive considering the unfavorable impact of forex in all regions, especially EMEA.

In the same way, its operating costs and expenses increased YoY because of its continued expansion and technological innovation. It served more customers, so it had to spend more. However, revenue growth easily offset it, which meant that its expansion remained fruitful. More interestingly, its costs and expenses decreased QoQ, which showed its improved efficiency. As a result, its operating margin improved to 32.2% versus 31.7% YoY and 24.3% QoQ.

Challenges

Inflation and intense competition remain the primary challenges faced by NFLX. Of course, these aspects affect its pricing flexibility and strategy to secure its customer base while protecting revenues and margins. This remains challenging today as inflation remains stubborn. In the US, it stays above the 2.0% target, which means that the Fed is still having a hard time keeping it under control. This may also be fueled by tariff wars and skyrocketing oil prices amid the Middle East tensions. With that, the budget or purchasing power of its customers may weaken. The production costs of films and series it produces and acquires may become more expensive, so it may incur higher cost pressures. It may have to raise wages and the marketing budget, so employees can keep up with inflation. It must also be careful with its pricing because a mistake can weaken viewership.

It must also be aware of the evolving competition. Aside from the traditional streaming platform competitors like Amazon Prime (AMZN) and Disney+ (DIS), it must also watch out for user-generated platforms like YouTube. Short-term video platforms like TikTok and social media reels must also be considered. I have no data to present, but since I'm always on the internet, I’d like to inform you of my observations. First is that reels have become more flexible; from less than one minute, they can be as long as 10-20 minutes. Second, I have noticed pages using AI narrators to summarize a film or an episode of a series.

Third, there are some pages or channels on YT and FB that are cutting films into multiple parts (say 10 minutes per video), so they won’t be flagged by the system. Users can watch the whole film. I will give you an example. This is a link to Halloween II (1981) on YT. As you can see, it’s divided into 10 parts. That way, this won’t be flagged by YT. Viewers can watch for free, while the videos of random uploaders can get engagements. Wise, isn’t it? Aside from that, many local producers have already created their channels and upload their films there. For example, Star Cinema, one of the biggest film producers in our country, has already uploaded over 700 videos on YT. This can be a challenge for NFLX and peers to penetrate other markets. Somehow, NFLX and peers also have to compete with Facebook and YT channels that upload videos with extremely and relatively cheaper costs.

Mitigants

Despite their potential risks, I believe that NFLX has a lot of strong tools to cushion their impact or avoid them.

One aspect to consider is content uniqueness. Not all films and series can be found on those free sites. Also, NFLX capitalizes on the quality and popularity of much of its content or videos, which cannot be watched on other platforms. This has been highlighted further from the pandemic years to today. Remember Squid Game and Money Heist? Their popularity broke all over the world and transcended multiple languages. These series prove Netflix’s ability to create global hits. Aside from content volume, it continues to create cultural moments.

In the US, NFLX continues to dominate the streaming market. Virgin River had 197.2M views and 18.4M viewers. What’s more interesting is that even old sitcoms are making a comeback to the mainstream market. And it seems that it still has a lot of loyal followers. Seinfeld ranked second with only 5.3M viewers. However, the number of views reached over 150M. This means that every viewer watched it 29 times during the month. Indeed, loyalty is not something that can be easily scaled or matched by peers. Also, the idea that eight of the 10 most streamed TV shows in the US are from Netflix proves its strong market position. The same situation happened in the UK. The Middle and Brooklyn Nine-Nine were among the shows with the fewest viewers. However, they topped the number of views.

Its content also appears data-driven. Why? NFLX seems to know what shows or films to greenlight and how to market them to capture viewers. Even thumbnail selection is done, if you ask me. As a viewer, I believe it also has this algorithm and data analytics that analyzes the viewing habits and watch time of every viewer. It also analyzes how much time or poor quality it takes for us to drop a movie or a series.

Moreover, its business model is evolving. From pure series and film subscriptions, NFLX also caters to gaming events and live sports. And to ensure customer retention even among the price-sensitive viewers, it also introduced ad-supported tiers and password-sharing crackdowns. It also has these mobile-only plans. This proves it can pivot quickly amid the evolving market and macroeconomic volatility.

Lastly, NFLX maintains a strong financial position to ensure robust liquidity. For instance, its cash and cash equivalents remain high at $12.3B. This is more than enough to cover all current liabilities. NFLX also shows prudent debt management considering its long maturity profile. Note that 92% of its borrowings will not mature within the year. More interestingly, its Net Debt/EBITDA is only 0.1x, which means that NFLX only needs 0.1 year, or just a month, to repay the whole amount even if it doesn’t have to. Hence, it has a large buffer to sustain its continued expansion and digital innovation.

Valuation

To reassess its valuation, I decided to update its P/E Ratio. As you can see, the stock now trades at 30.08x its EPS, which is a huge drop from the 43.35x multiple in 2025. This is also the second-lowest multiple in five or six years. And I believe the notable price drop has opened new buying opportunities for investors at a slight discount. It also trades below the average of 37.92x. If we use this to derive the fair value, the TP or target price, will be $117.54.

Now, an investor may say that it is too high to be a TP. However, it is also important to note that NFLX’s EPS had explosive growth in those years. In fact, it has already grown by 32.7%. If we use the PEG Ratio, we will get 0.92x (30.08/32.70). This means that NFLX is only trading at 92% of its earnings growth. It still has some buying room if you believe that the trading multiple should equate earnings growth. If we use this instead, the TP is $101.35. This proves that NFLX still has some upside, and that the recent selloff was overdone.

Technicals

NFLX’s technicals stay bullish despite the recent selloff. In fact, I believe this is an entry opportunity for interested investors. You can see that it did not break below the first key support level of $90.88. Instead, it flattened at $92-93, and we saw net buying volume at $93.10 last Wednesday. This can tell us that buyers took this as an opportunity to enter or increase their position. Additionally, it stays above the 50-day SMA, which means that the current momentum is still stronger than in the past 50 days. More interestingly, the 50-day SMA is trending upwards, which means that recent market sentiments have been optimistic.

Its MACD and RSI still adhere to my initial observation. You can see how the price high increased from $99 on March 5 to $108 on April 16. On the same dates, the highs of the two indicators also increased. This shows that more buyers still entered even if the stock price kept increasing. This also shows that bullish signals still persist today. Meanwhile, the MACD line is decreasing below the signal line, and the histogram is back to negative territory. This implies that selling volume has outpaced buying volume in line with the recent selloff. However, RSI shows that the selloff was due to overbuying. And now that buying room is wide, a price rebound is valid.

Conclusion

Netflix, Inc. continues to reign over the online streaming market with robust performance. This is supported by its solid customer base, in-demand content, and high liquidity that position it well against macroeconomic volatility and intense competition. Its valuation is also still reasonable at this level since the recent sell-off opened new buying opportunities. Technicals adhere to it as bullish signals persist. I’m reiterating my buy rating on this stock.

data.title: Goosehead Insurance: Secular Pressures Merit Caution
data.description:
Shares of Goosehead Insurance (GSHD) have been a poor performer over the past year, losing over half of their value. Shares have been weighed down by concerns about disintermediation from AI as well as the fact that customer retention has been weak, which could pose a long-term challenge. However, shares did rally 10% on Thursday as the company reported solid Q1 results. Additionally, it has made several changes in executive leadership, providing more clarity for investors. Still, I believe caution is merited.

In the company's first quarter, Goosehead Insurance earned $0.37, which beat estimates by $0.17 as revenue surged 23% to $93 million. Core revenue grew 15%, while contingent commissions surged 141% to $12 million. Adjusted EBITDA jumped 57% to $24 million as AI tools and increased revenue helped the business scale up, with margins hitting 26%, up from 20% a year ago. Employee compensation was up just 4%, speaking to the scalability of its model. During the quarter, it wrote $1.1 billion of premiums, up 13% from last year. The company now has 2 million policies in force, up 14%.

Goosehead is an insurance distributor, or agency, that sits in between customers and insurance companies. It focuses exclusively on personal lines, partnering with over 200 insurance companies like Chubb (CB), AIG (AIG), and others so that consumers can shop for the policy that best meets their needs. As you can see, it primarily focuses on home insurance, followed by auto policies, and then a smattering of other lines (like boats)

Goosehead is an independent insurance agency with ongoing franchise growth

To drive growth, Goosehead wants to steadily grow the number of affiliated agents that sell insurance to expand market share. It is making progress on this front. Corporate agent headcount was up 13% to 482 while franchise producers grew 3% to 2,150 (note the map below is as of Q4, which is why the number differs slightly). A primary goal of its corporate offering is to train agents to then open their own franchise, as it finds those founders perform much better than external hires.

There are three models for selling insurance currently. First, there is the Goosehead model, where an "independent" agency serves customers, offering quotes from multiple agencies. Second, there are companies like State Farm that have "captive" agencies. In other words, the insurer owns the agency, and that agency only sells their policies. The third option is for consumers to shop directly with the insurer. As you can see below, the independent channel through which Goosehead operates has grown, at the expense of captive agencies. The real growth, especially in auto, has been for consumers to shop directly with the insurance company, cutting out the middleman altogether.

This model faces risk from AI in my view

Frankly, I am very cautious about the outlook for agent-led models. Even before AI, consumers were shifting directly to insurance companies, particularly in auto, where policies are relatively commoditized and standard. If anything, AI agent tools will make it easier in the future to price shop, further reducing the need to go through an agent. The same is likely true for homeowners' policies, though I expect the direct share to remain structurally lower.

In general, the more customization a policy requires, the less likely simply shopping online can merit the best outcome. Particularly among higher-income homeowners, there may be more unique situations where antiques or valuables also need to be insured, or someone owns multiple homes. Here, the human-touch and a "white glove" service model are likely to endure more. Over the next ten years, I expect direct market share to rise across both product lines. Over the past decade, independents have grown at the expense of captives, but as direct continues to grow, I fear that their market share may also come under strain.

Goosehead's retention has been poor

Beyond this, retention improvement has been slow. It has now sat at 85% for three straight quarters, after spending the previous five at 84%. It expects client retention to reach 86% at some point this year, but it is a slow journey. This continues to run below historic levels, and years of significant insurance premium inflation may be leading to increased price shopping by consumers and lower retention.

However, beyond this, its "net promoter score"("NPS") has also fallen. That speaks to lower customer satisfaction, which may be playing a role in weaker retention. Frankly, some of this drop may be tied to years of premium inflation. Higher prices may be annoying customers, driving down their satisfaction scores, through no fault of Goosehead. However, it is also a warning sign that quality is degrading, especially as it works to automate work previously completed by people. It has been a significant fall, from 87 to 72, and that has come as premium inflation has slowed a bit. As such, I am concerned it speaks to degrading client service.

Lily, its AI virtual assistant, is now handling 19% of all inbound calls, reducing the need for live agents. As AI gets better, that share should only increase, helping to drive incremental cost savings and expanding margins. However, if these interactions are playing a role in lower NPS scores, they could also end up detracting from revenue and not end up being a net positive to the bottom line.

Its financial position is sound

Turning to its balance sheet, Goosehead ended the quarter with $26 million of cash and $328 million of notes payable. This leaves the company with about 2.5x debt/EBITDA leverage. Given its cash flow is fairly predictable (franchise fees and commissions), this is a very reasonable amount of leverage to carry. Free cash flow was $23 million in the quarter, and Goosehead spent $50 million on share buybacks. I expect buybacks to continue.

Alongside results, Goosehead announced it has hired John Martin as CFO; he previously was CFO of an e-commerce company. It also promoted Mark Jones to be COO; Jones was previously CFO. This would seemingly position Jones as the next CEO as part of a multiyear succession plan, which appears well structured. Given the promotion of the former CEO, I do not expect this change to shift the company's financial policy.

Looking forward, management affirmed full year guidance, which includes organic growth of 10-19% and total written premium growth of 12-20%. With 13% more franchisees and modest premium inflation, this guidance appears reasonable, and the company should earn at least $2.20 this year. That leaves shares at about 23x earnings, which is not particularly expensive for a company with mid-teens revenue growth and margin expansion if sustained.

I would avoid Goosehead given AI risk

Ultimately, the question is how one views the long-term outcome of AI. If one believes Goosehead can use AI to cut out people costs and enhance margins, it is a net winner. However, if one believes AI can eventually crowd out agency market share (or at the very least deflate the commission rate agents earn), then Goosehead is likely a secular loser. Given its own weakening NPS score, I question how much of a winner it will be, and I do believe that the independent channel is likely to lose market share over time. Goosehead may be able to grow its share within independent channels to offset this secular pressure, but to me it faces pressure.

I am not inclined to buy shares of a stock at 23x earnings when facing a secular headwind, and I worry about growth decelerating in coming years, which will further pressure its multiple. As such, I recommend using this relief rally to exit Goosehead. I view shares as a "sell" and view this as a name to avoid.

data.title: Prenetics Global: Quarterly Subscription Focus Could Turn $120M AAR Into A Billion-Dollar Machine
data.description:
Introduction and Thesis

I am strongly bullish on Prenetics Global Limited (PRE), driven by a strategic shift to quarterly subscriptions, which has doubled customer value from $110 in 2025 to $233 in early 2026. In the recently released Q4 2025, it also indicates this optimism by maintaining an 80% new customer subscription rate. This is mainly driven by its new product, IM8, which in 12 months achieved annualized recurring revenue of $120 million. It was possible because IM8 revenues in 2025 started at $581,000 in the first month and reached $10 million per month. For those who don’t know, IM8 is a health and wellness multivitamin by Prenetics, which provides a clinical-grade nutritional supplement with fruity flavors and bioactive vitamins. They are designed to boost resilience, energy, and longevity.

The management reaffirms revenue guidance for FY2026 of $180 million to $200 million, with an ARR path of $250 million to $300 million by the end of 2026. If you take a midpoint revenue guidance of $190 million, that implies a possible revenue growth YoY of 105.65%. In my view, IM8 is a revenue machine that could drive Prenetics to a billion-dollar company for one reason. That consumers are increasingly curious, informed, and intentional in seeking food that supports mental clarity, longevity, and emotional well-being.

Globally, around two-thirds of consumers take vitamins to supplement their diets. Right now, only 17% of consumers do not use any supplements. I think this is what is driving the global dietary supplement market from its current volume of $229.77 million to $507.35 million in 2035 at a CAGR of 9.2%. Now, let’s look at this from a deeper perspective and unravel why I believe IM8 warrants a bullish outlook for Prenetics.

Company Brief

Prenetics Global Limited provides advanced consumer health services internationally through IM8 wellness products. The company also provides Europa sports nutrition distribution and CircleDNA genetic testing using next-generation sequencing technology, among others. It was incorporated in 2014 and is headquartered in Hong Kong.

IM8 Business Behind The Surge in Quarterly Subscriptions

I want to delve deeper into Prenetics IM8, but before that, let’s see what the overall market has in store for this company. In my previous explanations, I introduced the market growth expectations for dietary supplements, which are projected to grow at a CAGR of 9.2% between 2026 and 2035.

Well, this is not by chance. It is by design, particularly when I look at the drivers of this growth, among them being consumers being more intentional, curious, and informed. In my mind, when I see the growth trajectory Prenetics is recording, such as an 80% subscription rate, what comes into my mind is how this comes to be. I believe the market is everything when it comes to product market acceptance. Therefore, when personalized nutrition becomes part of everyday life as opposed to an exclusive privilege of private clinics, elite athletes, and customized diet plans, then something to do with consumer awareness must have shifted.

The shift is evident in 84% of U.S. consumers who now prioritize health and wellness, similar to 94% and 70% in China and the United Kingdom, respectively. In these figures, good health and wellness are seen from six angles: sleep, fitness, better health, mindfulness, appearance, and nutrition. In fact, millennials and Gen Z are the demographics becoming more intentional about their health, which is why this market, from an external view, is likely to see a sustained upside momentum in the long run.

Since I previously introduced IM8 and what it entails, let me go straight to how Prenetics is positioning itself in this market, looking at the quarterly subscription. In December 2025, PRE introduced a quarterly subscription in the U.S., where subscribers no longer pay $89 per month for 30-day supplies; customers can now pay $78 per month. This amount is now billed quarterly, meaning a customer pays an upfront fee of $235 for three months’ supplies. It is a win-win strategy that saves a customer $10 per month and also allows PRE to collect 2x to 3x upfront cash per customer. This saves the company by allowing shipping to be done only once, reducing fulfillment costs.

The impact is evident in the improved average order value, which has increased from $133 in Q4 2025 to $233 by the end of February 2026. This comes at 75.2%, indicating that more people are buying into this win-win strategy.

My question is whether PRE can maintain this growth trajectory. In my view, two factors can sustain this impressive growth: market tailwinds and the model strategy itself. I highlighted the market tailwinds, previously on the consumer awareness driven by millennials and Gen Z, but just to move on to something else, PRE is doing well internationally. In FY2025, 60% of the revenues were generated outside the U.S., which reveals that the company has a global market acceptance.

Regarding the quarterly subscription model, there is evidence of significant growth in average order value after it was introduced in December 2025 in the U.S. Note that today, about 76% to 77% of Americans use supplements daily. Therefore, when PRE records 39.7% of its revenues from the U.S., it shows that the company is gaining a strong market reach in this demographic. In fact, most of the popular supplements used among Americans are multivitamins, with upwards of 58% of Americans using them. To me, this indicates that consumers are becoming more intentional about their wellness and are considering healthy living. As a result, I believe PRE has positioned itself to offer these dietary supplements to cater to these needs. This is also reflected well in the financials and valuation below.

Optimistic About Q1 2026

As we head to Q1 2026, I remain optimistic about customer subscriptions. PRE has set a strong precedent, and the market dynamics point to a sustainable uptrend. Owing to the pivot towards promoting quarterly subscriptions, the change has proven to drive a meaningful step up in blended average order value, reportedly more than double in some metrics, while reducing logistics costs. If the momentum holds, which I believe it will due to the strong market growth, Q1 should reflect further stabilization or modest improvement in subscription metrics supporting higher recurring revenue visibility.

On the bottom line, I foresee a gradual improvement. PRE has demonstrated strong operating leverage in recent quarters, with adjusted EBITDA losses narrowing significantly from over $6 million in Q1 2025 to around $2 million by Q3 2025, although it skipped higher in Q4 for reasons I will explain in the financial section shortly. Although Q1 2026 may still have elevated losses due to continued marketing investment and seasonal factors, I believe the combination of higher average order values, efficient customer acquisition, and the ongoing divestiture of noncore assets should help narrow the gap towards profitability.

Financials and Valuation

IM8 is driving impressive top-line growth for PRE as evidenced by its revenue trend from 2021 to 2025. Since 2021, revenues have grown by almost 7.37x to $92.39 million in FY2025. But what I am noticing here is that the most significant growth occurred after PRE introduced IM8 in September 2024, which is roughly the end of FY2024. Therefore, the impact of IM8’s launch was felt in FY2025, with revenue rising from $30.62 million to $92.39 million, representing unprecedented YoY revenue growth of 201.72%. Given my explanations above about consumers being more intentional and informed, I believe management’s revenue guidance of $180 million to $200 million in FY2026 is possible. This could represent YoY revenue growth of 105.7%, in line with the consensus analysts’ forecast of 106.61% YoY revenue growth in FY2026.

From a bottom-line perspective, PRE continues to reveal that it is gradually performing well, even though it is still a loss-making company. For example, from FY2022, the net loss has been declining YoY through FY2025. Apart from FY2025, which recorded a slight increase in the net loss, driven by selling and marketing expenses. I think this is not a reason to worry because, with an increase in selling and marketing expenses from $1.6 million in Q4 2024 to $16.1 million in Q4 2025, the company achieved significant global market access for its IM8 product. Most importantly, selling and expenses in Q4 2025 accounted for 44.0% of the total revenues, revealing that Prenetics is investing in high-return customer acquisition, resulting in an average order value of $233.

EPS is also showing this gradual improvement of the company’s performance, given that in FY2025, PRE’s EPS came in at -$4.15, missing the analyst’s estimate of -$2.54. This huge miss is a direct result of investing most of its earnings back into selling and marketing. Now, this is expected to rebound EPS from -$4.15 to -$2.57 in FY2026, representing 38.15% YoY growth.

From a valuation perspective, I will compare PRE’s peers, USANA Health Sciences, Inc. (USNA) and LifeVantage Corporation (LFVN), to assess how they compare in terms of multivitamin sales. PRE’s PS stands at 2.71x, well above its peers, USNA at 0.40x and LFVN at 0.28x. This gives the impression of an overvalued company, but I think it is justified when compared to peer forward revenue growth projections for FY2026. PRE leads by a wide margin, 114.74%, followed by USNA at 5.42% and LFVN at -1.53%. This is a testament to PRE’s dietary supplements’ strong market acceptance compared to its peers, which I think will drive this company into profitability.

Investment Risks

Sustained Operating Loss

PRE continues to record improved adjusted EBITDA with Q4 2025 at 70% YoY upside, but GAAP loss of $55 million in FY2025. In fact, the company projects possible net profit to be recorded in Q4 2027, assuming the revenue trajectory continues with the current momentum. I therefore think there is a risk if marketing expenses are reduced, which could slow down its subscriber reach. However, it guides marketing expenses to about 44%-50% of revenue, representing a significant investment to reach long-term, high-order value customers. If this spend fails to convert into long-term, high-order value customers, PRE is at risk of financial constraints and prolonged profitability.

Conclusion

I have a bullish rating on PRE driven by the quarterly subscription strategy, which is already paying off. The company has market backing from a well-informed and intentional group in making individual health and wellness decisions.
